Framework · free to use

Start a gym vs buy an existing one.

The buy-vs-build decision for aspiring fitness operators. Real cost comparison, what you get with each, the financing dynamics, and how the AI-native operational model changes the math in 2026.

By Bill Colbert · Founder, Treetop Growth Strategy
Published May 2026 · More from the library
Quick answer

The trade-off in one table

FactorStart from scratchBuy existing
Upfront capital\$80K-\$1.5M+ (typically lower for small format)\$200K-\$5M+ (includes goodwill / member base value)
Time to revenue6-12 monthsDay 1
Member acquisitionYou do it allInherit existing base
Brand controlFullConstrained by inherited brand
Tech / systems flexibilityBuild from scratch (advantage in 2026)Often inherit legacy systems
Risk profileHigher (concept unproven locally)Lower (existing P&L)
Owner energy requiredHigher earlyLower early; higher mid-term
Multiplier you payNone2-5x annual EBITDA typically
When starting from scratch makes sense

Five scenarios that favor building

  • You have an existing client base / coach reputation. Trainer-to-owner transitions are uniquely well-suited to launching new.
  • You want full brand and operational control. Build the concept you actually want.
  • You want to build agentic-from-day-one operations. Buying an existing gym means inheriting legacy software stacks and staff models that may resist AI-native operational changes.
  • Your market has no quality acquisition targets. Sometimes there's nothing worth buying in your geography.
  • Capital is tighter than time. Building lean takes less capital than buying.
When buying existing makes sense

Five scenarios that favor acquiring

  • You want revenue from day one. The biggest reason to buy.
  • You're risk-averse on concept-market fit. Existing P&L is proven (review carefully).
  • You're acquiring as part of a multi-unit roll-up. Buying multiple existing locations + applying operational excellence (including AI-native upgrades) is a strong consolidation play.
  • The seller is motivated and the price reflects it. Distress sales sometimes offer real value.
  • You can credibly run / improve the operation. Don't buy what you can't operate.
The AI-native angle that changes 2026 math

Why this matters more now

In 2025-2026, the operational gap between AI-native gym operations and legacy operations widened dramatically. Implications for the buy-vs-build decision:

  • Acquiring an existing gym often means acquiring its legacy operational debt. Old CRM, manual lead-nurture workflows, multi-person front-desk staff models. Upgrading to AI-native operations post-acquisition is often a 12-18 month project on its own.
  • Starting fresh lets you build agentic from day one. An AI-native new gym deployment with fitagentic.ai from the lease signing onward typically operates at 30-50% lower staffing overhead than the equivalent legacy operation.
  • For roll-up acquirers, the playbook is now: acquire existing operations at reasonable multiples, then aggressively apply AI-native operational upgrades — that's where the post-acquisition value creation happens.
Cost comparison: small boutique studio

Concrete example

Start from scratch

  • Facility, equipment, licensing, software, marketing, working capital: \$120K-\$280K
  • Time to revenue: 6-9 months
  • Member acquisition: do it yourself
  • Year-1 owner take-home: typically minimal to modest

Buy existing comparable studio

  • Purchase price at typical 2-3x EBITDA multiple: \$200K-\$600K depending on size and profitability
  • Down payment (typically 20-30%): \$50K-\$180K
  • SBA financing on the rest: ~10-year amortization
  • Time to revenue: day 1
  • Year-1 owner take-home: depends entirely on the acquired business's existing P&L
Diligence checklist for buyers

What to verify before signing

  • Real member count (vs claimed) — pull access logs not member lists
  • Real churn rate (last 12 months)
  • Member-to-revenue concentration (is one founding member 30% of revenue?)
  • Lease terms and remaining duration
  • Equipment age and condition (depreciation reality)
  • Existing software contracts (assignment terms)
  • Staff retention if key staff are part of the value
  • Reason for sale (real reason — not the stated reason)
  • Pending or threatened litigation
  • Recent online reviews and reputation
Companion tools

Build your decision

  • Cost to Start a Gym (full guide)
  • Gym Startup Cost Calculator
  • AI for Multi-Unit Fitness Operators Playbook — relevant for roll-up acquirers.
What you'll deploy on day one
The operational layer the AI-native model is built on.

The cost math above assumes AI agents handle lead nurture, no-show recovery, member retention monitoring, and member-facing coaching. Here's what that looks like in practice — the workflows fitagentic.ai is purpose-built for.
